content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. DUNE : Part 2 - what a watch !

**Score:** 58
**Body:**  I am a bit late to the wagon with the missed opportunity to see it on the big screen but my God! This is the most visually stunning movie I have ever seen. I have been intrigued by the storyline but never expected the movie to be such a fine piece of cinema. The cinematography, the effects and the breathtaking music draws you in like never before. 


My fellow redditors please go and watch this movie. Some things are meant to be admired and this surely is one of them. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Movies like this (sarcastically) piss me off because I was so immersed and I didn’t want it to end. I swear I wanted to fight for Paul after that cave speech. I wish I could go back and watch it again with fresh eyes.
  - Yess exactly! I was immersed for 4-5 hours (part 1 & 2) what do I do now ?
    - Good news is the third movie is confirmed and scheduled to come out Dec of this year. I started reading the books after part two. I didn’t finish because I went down some crazy Lore rabbit holes on YouTube. A lot of high quality videos by the fandom about the world. It’s so interesting esp remembering this was all written half a century ago.
2. I felt like the use of the camera to build epic sequences was really admirable and cinematic. The emotional arc of the characters (in spite of a really entertaining villain turn by Austin Butler) was a bit less convincing. But if you're into great cinematography, it's a must-see. 
3. Not sure if you’ve seen all of Denis’s movies but if you haven’t go watch them now. Multiple times.
  - Arrival - Blade Runner 2049 - Dune 1-2

Denis Villeneuve became like, a legendary director of sci-fi in just a few years. His previous films are amazing too, but his recent dip into sci-fi has been incredible.
  - I still hold Blade Runner 2049 as the mose beautiful film I've ever seen. Every single frame of it could be blown up, framed, hung on a wall and be art.
4. It hits better on big screen !
5. Freaking incredible film
6. I've watched this movie like 6 times. It just gets better and better. Every scene is followed up with an amazing scene. Just amazing. Great cast...great acting. Great story.  Great cinematography.  Great soundtrack. Great sound design.

## 2. I’m looking for movies that show the brutal reality of what Nazi Germany did.

**Score:** 54
**Body:** Here’s a brief rundown of the situation. I have a nineteen year old roommate who is clueless about things, and recently found out he’s been listening to Nazi metal and reading Nazi literature. Unfortunately kicking him out is not a current option. Instead I plan on having watch some movies showing him brutal reality of what he’s getting into. I already plan on having him watch American History X to show him what that mindset can have on modern society, but I also want to have him watch things that will basically scare him off the idea altogether. I don’t want to show him heavily fictionalized stuff like Inglorious Basterds or Jojo Rabbit. I’m thinking more like God on Trial. Any legit suggestions would be appreciated.

Also, please keep it related to the topic. NO suggestions that talk about the current US administration. Thanks.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Schindler's List?
  - I agree with Schindler’s List, it’s pretty fast paced for a long movie, and it’s ‘fair’, in that it shows that Oskar Schindler wasn’t perfect but that people can ‘learn’ humanity even in the face of danger..
2. Look at the OG Judgement At Nuremberg, it contains the some of the original footage shot by the soldiers who liberated the camps.  Not for the faint of heart. 
3. The Pianist.
4. Shoah. You probably won’t even have to get past the first hour.

I wouldn’t recommend American History X. It’s a film with a substantial neo-nazi following. Made the mistake of making what it was trying to condemn look too cool.
5. Come and See
6. Conspiracy - very important film
7. [deleted]
8. For what you are requesting, I would highly suggest not to show a fictional film about that subject but a documentary about it. The most famous one is a french documentary from 1956 of 30 minutes named Night & Fog and it’s just a narrator description of what the allies found in the camps when they arrived and it’s a mixture of photos, films and testimony. It’s short, straight to the point and explained clearly what they witnessed. Once you see the mountains of bones that they stocked to use as fertilizer during the Nazi regime, you get a good idea of what they were capable of. I have watched it for free on YouTube, check for a version with English subtitles or dubbed if you wish. Good luck.
9. The Pianist

## 3. Prisoners (2013)

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Slow burn, but worth the patience.

Not for casual watching. A gripping thriller that keeps you thinking long after it ends. If you like tense, well-acted thrillers, this one is a must watch.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Every single film by Denis Villeneuve is now a must-watch
  - Indeed, I was skeptical about Dune but after watching them I really liked them
2. That movie absolutely destroyed me emotionally. Hugh Jackman's performance was unreal - you could feel his desperation through the screen.
  - I feel you 😅 Hugh Jackman absolutely kills it. The way he conveys desperation is insane. That movie sticks with you for days. 

3. Yeah that movie just sits heavy the whole time, like the tension never really lets you breathe. Hugh Jackman felt way more raw than I expected going in. Definitely not a “put it on in the background” kind of watch.
4. One of my favorites
5. It's a great film. A little ridiculous at times, and a little too hammy and sentimental at other times, but the acting is just too good so you're willing to suspend disbelief.
6. I found the writing to be a bit clunky. “Hi son, I can’t afford car payments because I’m not getting enough carpentry contracts at my carpentry job”

Why would he have to explain to his son what job he has? It makes no sense. The reason the line is there is so that later when he builds something out of wood you know he has skills, but you shouldn’t just make him say it, show a truck with a carpentry logo on it.

Imagine, “bye son, I’m off to work at the police station because I’m a police officer”

Clunky.
7. Loved it!
8. I believe Weapons (2025) is a (very good, very soft) parody of Prisoners.

The director of Weapons acknowledged the influence, but there are so many little details shared between the two films, from the look of the town/ neighborhood and the occupation and vehicle of one of the main characters

## 4. My boyfriend isn’t afraid of ghost movies anymore — need actually scary haunted movie suggestions

**Score:** 18
**Body:** So my boyfriend used to get scared pretty easily with ghost/haunted movies, but lately he’s been acting way too brave 😅 We’ve watched a bunch of the popular ones and now he claims nothing fazes him anymore.

I want to prove him wrong — looking for genuinely creepy haunted/ghost movie recommendations. The kind that sticks in your head after the credits roll, not just jump-scare spam. Old, new, international — anything works as long as it’s truly unsettling.

Upvotes are appreciated so more horror fans can weigh in, but mostly I just want some solid suggestions 🙏

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Changling (1980)
2. Terrified (2017) Original Spanish title: Aterrados
3. The Entity (1982)
4. Burnt Offerings (1976) 
5. The Ring (I personally prefer the American version)

The Shining

The Others
 
It Follows

The Grudge

The Woman in Black

The Conjuring

The Sixth Sense

Oculus

A Tale of Two Sisters

And some *almost* ghost stories:

Hereditary

The Wailing

The Blair Witch Project

Session 9
6. Lake Mungo had a scene that still haunts me.

Pulse (Japanese version)

There’s a series of shorts called Tales of Terror From Tokyo. Some of them are on YouTube, and most are worth checking out, but The Visitor in particular is good.
7. The Haunting  1963  is still the scariest haunted house movie I've ever seen
8. Event Horizon
9. The Orphanage


The film isn't just scary, it's very sad and disturbing, and therefore stays with you for a very long time.
10. Ghost Story is pretty good/creepy.  Old school — made in the early 80s I think? 

## 5. Looking for a movie that will genuinely make me want to start living my life

**Score:** 12
**Body:** Basically, I'm 20 years old and studying abroad in Italy right now, but I cannot seem to fully enjoy the experience because I'm always worried about hangxiety, my future, what people think of me etc...

I need a movie that will make me think: LET GO AND LIVE! Chat GPT recommended the Secret Life of Walter Mitty, but I'm not a huge Ben Stiller fan (I know that's being picky).

I'm open to anything and everything else! Thank you!

EDIT: Okay guys, you got me. Tonight I will watch the Secret Life of Walter Mitty! Check back in in 2 hrs!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Good Will Hunting

Also i don’t usually care for Ben Stiller either, but Walter Mitty is a good movie
2. Pleasantville
3. Harold and Maude
4. Swingers (1996)
  - So what you are saying is that OP is money and that they don't even know it?
5. O.m.g. OP, you *are already in Italy!* Please go out and explore where you are!!  You can watch a movie some other time.
6. Finding Forrester

Amélie
7. Defending Your Life is funny and definitely inspiring if you're worried (which is based on fear) etc.
8. The start of Romancing The Stone.  Despite your fears, get on that plane and go do something insane.
9. Billy Elliot (the film, but even more so the 2014 live musical recording)

## 2. What is the realistic gunfight scene in movie that not from Heat?

**Score:** 505

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sicario

Children of Men

And while it's not a movie, the Embassy seige in Homeland was very well done.
  - One more for sicario. The scene at the border is just awesome.
2. The Way of the Gun
3. Ronin.

Doubles as one of the best car chase movies of all time, too.
4. For my fellow pistol nerds, Tom Cruise in [Collateral](https://youtu.be/oEFPcljAXgs?si=zC5i6YuKfbIMSrl4).
  - Yo homie, is that my briefcase?
    - ![gif](giphy|DthDMLI8TCwwAJPhNQ)
  - Same director as Heat.
5. Equilibrium. Duh.

https://preview.redd.it/tfkjx0oukfig1.jpeg?width=350&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=eba20f9cdeb8287eedcd6d00f5f16ec012d04620
  - Gunkata

## 3. Why did Tommy Wiseau's legendary film cost $6 million while Nolan's debut film cost $6,000?

**Score:** 498
**Body:** Tommy Wiseau seems to be quite good at making films.🧐

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Because Nolan shot *Following* on black and white 16mm, which he would’ve chosen because it’s very cheap and easy, whereas Wiseau is the inventor of a pioneering (and now extinct) cinematographic technique involving shooting both digital and film at the same time, in colour. He did this because he found out nobody ever had. Nobody ever has because it’s nonsense. You can’t light a scene for both. You have to do everything twice and then throw all of one of them away, because they don’t mix. Colour is expensive and he did it twice, for no reason. The entire production was a similar bonfire. 

I believe it’s part of the reason nobody stepped in creatively: every part of the production process was just as incomprehensible as the story to anybody familiar with filmmaking. Everybody just did what they were told, no matter how insane. Nobody rode to even a partial rescue. We get pure Tommy.

If you wanted to repeat the formula, for a reality show or something, you’d need to give spirited oddballs experienced film crews who strictly weren’t allowed to help at all. They just had to carry out instructions. You’d get all sorts of *The Rooms*.
  - Plus there was the nonsense of building a rooftop set in a parking lot with greenscreen.
  - I believe he also purchased the cameras, while most would lease.
  - There's a British prank show where the main guy in it dresses up as different obnoxious characters to prank people. He managed to get a job on a Tommy Wiseau movie set, and he tries to get Tommy to spend some of the movie budget on Lotto tickets. So they can win money to put towards the movie production costs 😅
2. $6M according to Wiseau? Yeah I trust him
  - you are tearing me APART pancyfantz!

anyways, how is your sex life?
    - Oh hi 00ishmael00, I didn’t know it was you. Oh Hai doggy. You’re my favourite customer. 
3. I mean.... Which do you remember and still makes money in theaters?
  - I, and I’m sure many others, remember The Room much more fondly than Following. I wouldn’t be surprised if The Room made more money, as it is still being screened and is a cult classic.
4. Becasue Wiseau had no idea what he was doing.

## 4. “What’s a movie that trusted the audience a little too much?”

**Score:** 463
**Body:** I’m talking about films that deliberately withhold exposition, explanations, or emotional cues.

Sometimes that restraint is powerful. Other times it feels like the film is daring you to pretend you understood it. Take Tenet for example 

Where do you draw the line between subtle and frustrating?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Primer (2004) is another good one
  - [Obligatory xkcd reference](https://xkcd.com/657/large/)
    - I'd like to see the Coherence graph for that one. 
2. I live in a country where every English movie is shown with subtitles. So when I watched Tenet in Imax with my best friend, we both had a blast. Even after the movie was over, we spent so much time discussing.
So I was really confused when I went online and saw people complaining about the sound and other things.
3. I literally could not hear this fucking movie. 
4. 2001 A Space Odyssey

Rock Hudson was reported to say: “Will someone tell me what the hell this is about?”

____

It's one of my favorite films. And I get the criticism. There's a lot that goes unsaid. It's not a standard narrative movie. It's not even a standard Stanley Kubrick movie. The frame of reference is, itself. It's uniqueness is intentional, even if it's off-putting. 

I won't defend it ad nauseum. Nor will I browbeat someone by saying (Rick & Morty-style) that they're too stupid to understand its meanings. Give it a try. If you don't like it, fine. I like other movies, too. Regardless, it's probably one of the best movies ever.
  - I do think the plot is meant to be felt rather than explained.

But isn't it at least clear it's about the ascension of humans? From dumb apes to space faring gods? If you understand that much then you basically understand the movie.
    - It’s about the ascension of humans primarily, not necessarily into “gods” though. I think it’s intent is to communicate a reverence and awe for our species’ potential. Celebrating our nature to forever seek further understanding (represented by the monoliths) while positing that we as a species are infants on the universal stage. We’re barely beginning to interact with space outside of our planet.

I think it is the greatest movie ever made and it’s a trip. Very hopeful that we don’t squander our potential
5. I'm possibly catastrophically stupid, but I couldn't get my head around Looper.
  - Never expect a movie with a paradox to make sense, just enjoy the ride.

## 5. A Bug’s Life is such a good film, and it has some pretty powerful scenes. This one is my favorite.

**Score:** 422

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's definitely one of the best versions of Seven Samurai. :-)  

Right up there with The Three Amigos and Galaxy Quest.
  - May I remind you he's wearing a costume, not a uniform.
2. The original “We are the 99%”

Kevin Spacey voicing a bad guy that preys on and takes advantage of those weaker than him has also aged… appropriately
3. I love the one where hopper explains "you let one ant stand up to us" and what can happen, to his allies.
4. No war but class war
5. Bugs life is top notch, best Pixar era
6. "No, remember Ma!"
7. https://preview.redd.it/so0m3pfd1jig1.jpeg?width=640&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=e3744f543223cac40d6b170a70c2161cb17cdee2
8. Can we return bugs life to the fore or western culture?
9. Fine, I guess I'll rewatch this classic.

Pixar cooked so hard with some of their movies back then. Heck, Geri's Game still gets a periodic rewatch from me since it's so good. A short, but still good.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. My top 20 Asian movies of 2025

**Score:** 60
**Body:** I know some people think that 2025 was a weak year for Asian cinema, but there were some real gems, especially in the indie and arthouse scene. Some of these technically didn't release in 2025, but they only became available to me (in the US) last year. I haven't watched everything yet (*Two Seasons, Two Strangers*, *Human Resource*), which might shake up the list. Here are my thoughts on the ones I did see:

1. *It Was Just an Accident*   
Jafar Panahi is my favorite Iranian director, so my expectations were sky high, especially after it won the Palme d'Or. Somehow, it exceeded them. It's simultaneously a taut thriller, a found family drama, a dark comedy, a moral dilemma, and a biting anti-authoritarian treatise. Despite its heavy social and political themes, nothing feels preachy. Every character is treated as if they are the protagonist, every perspective given room to breathe, and Panahi lets his ensemble cast drive the narrative. Most notably, there's an intense single-shot scene that showcases the range and complexity of his actors and the characters they portray, postmarked by an ambiguous and haunting conclusion. This film is deeply Iranian and personal to the director, but in that pursuit of specificity, Panahi creates something universal. Shot on a tight budget and filmed in secrecy, it cements Panahi as one of the greatest living auteurs. **5/5**  
  
2. *All We Imagine as Light*   
Ethereal and dreamlike, but with two feet firmly planted on the ground. Nothing here is contrived, as if the characters have always been alive and will continue to be once the runtime ends. Kapadia handles the material with a subtle grace that contemporaries with twice the experience and accolades can't match, supported by two quietly devastating central performances. A triumph for Indian cinema. **4.5/5**

3. *Kokuho*   
"He'll leave nothing except his art when he dies." What matters more, pedigree or passion? What would you sacrifice to attain greatness? Is legacy more important than lived experience? These are the questions at the heart of Kokuho, the latest epic from director Lee Sang-il. Spanning generations, the story follows the son of a Yakuza boss, who, after his father's murder, devotes his life to Kabuki theater under the tutelage of a famous master. As a zainichi, a foreigner in his own home, Lee brings a rare perspective. Close enough to depict Kabuki authentically, yet distant enough to view it with clarity. Kokuho is both a deconstruction of Kabuki and a love letter to it, drawing parallels to the yakuza, institutions built on a foundation of nationalism, hierarchy, repression, and ritual. But its true strength lies in what it strips away. The makeup. The costumes. The tattoos. The ceremony. What remains when the performance ends? That question, and the film's refusal to answer it, are what elevate Kokuho to greatness. **4.5/5**

4. *The World of Love*   
Along with July Jung and Bora Kim, Yoo Ga-eun is one of the three best emerging directors from South Korea, in my opinion. Few filmmakers can capture the fleeting nature of childhood with the same accuracy and subtlety. With her third feature, World of Love, she ascends to the top of that list. I won't discuss the plot for fear of spoilers, but the film tackles heavier themes than her previous works, while retaining the warmth those films are adored for. Newcomer Seo Soo-bin gives the best debut performance I've seen in years. A star in the making. Character-driven, earnest, raw, yet deeply endearing. This is the best film to emerge from South Korea in 2025, and frankly, one of the best films of the year, period. **4.5/5**

5. *Viet and Nam*   
"Leave the light on. I'll dream more easily." Banned in Vietnam for its "negative" portrayal of the country and its people, it's even more justification for why the film deserves a wider audience. Reminiscent of the avant-garde restraint of the works of Apichatpong Weerasethakul, the film is intimately shot on 16mm, with long, unbroken takes and poetic dialogue rich with haunting symbolism. It follows the fleeting romance of two coal miners searching for the remains of one of their fathers lost to the war. A meditation on inherited trauma, mirroring a wounded nation's unresolved need for closure. It reminds me of a favorite quote from the Watchmen series, "You can't heal under a mask, Angela... Wounds need air." **4.5/5**

6. *Brief History of a Family*   
*The Talented Mr. Ripley* meets *Saltburn*, but a better film than both, especially the latter. There's a lot of sumptuous visual symbolism to chew on, bolstered by a pulsing synth score and strong social and cultural themes centered on China's one-child policy. However, beneath the glossy exterior lies something far more sinister upon closer examination. An impressive feature debut from Lin Jianjie. The ambiguous ending will polarize some audiences, but to me, it was well earned. **4.5/5**

7. *Happyend*   
"In the end, all we can do is appeal to the conscience of those in power... It's frustrating to think that's all we can do." A biting sci-fi dystopian drama, Happyend wisely holds its genre conventions close to its chest. Reminiscent of the dystopian restraint of *Plan 75*, crossed with the hopeful endnote of Haneke’s *Caché*, the sci-fi elements loom in the background, in what essentially amounts to a high school hangout movie. Happyend asks the right questions without offering any real answers. At its worst, it comes off as sophomoric and coldly calculated, but these are nitpicks. An increasingly relevant cautionary tale about the first steps towards totalitarianism, and the naive, but progressive youth who fight back against it on their own terms. **4/5**

8. *Renoir*   
"We cry when people die. Do we cry because we feel sorry for the dead? Or because we feel sorry for ourselves?" The film's greatest weakness is that it lacks a traditional emotional anchor, which is also its greatest strength. Hayakawa keeps the viewer at arm's length, challenging us to make our own conclusions. As the title suggests, much like an impressionist painting, it's not trying to portray reality, but evoke the feeling of reality through short strokes. Here, seemingly mundane, disturbing, and fantastical vignettes of a girl’s life as she grapples with her father’s terminal illness, the rift it creates within her family, and the quiet loneliness that settles over them all. Individually, they feel meandering and disjointed, but as a whole, they paint a picture. Challenging, but rewarding. **4/5**

9. *Homebound*   
"When I say my name, people look down on me. If I hide it, I look down on myself." A heartfelt story of two childhood friends from a rural village and their enduring brotherhood against the tides of societal, cultural, and natural forces. Homebound surprised me. The emotional ending didn't quite resonate with me until I later learned it was inspired by a true story about two friends' journey home during the COVID lockdown. Sentimental, but still grounded in reality, it's proof that you can have your cake and eat it too. Homebound understands that where we come from matters as much as where we're going. An emphatic success that will appeal to general audiences. Now, if you'll excuse me, I'm off to find biryani. **4/5**

10. *Resurrection*   
A visual spectacle of epic scope. Bi Gan's latest does not disappoint. Unapologetically enigmatic and abstract, spanning multiple genres and decades, a Lynchian fever dream catered towards cinephiles and arthouse enthusiasts. A meta film that asks the viewer to surrender to its imagery rather than its narrative. Attempts at decoding it only lead to frustration, made heavier by its nearly three-hour runtime. A film I admired more than I adored. **4/5**

11. *Love in the Big City*   
"Some people aren't just part of your story, they are the story." I almost passed on this one. A formulaic rom-com adapted from a K-drama? No Thanks. What I got instead was something fresh, sincere, and genuinely funny. Think *Castaway on the Moon* meets *Us and Them* (2018). Set in the heart of Seoul, the story follows the friendship between a closeted gay man and a free-spirited woman as they stumble through adulthood together. From nabbing a panty-stealing pervert, to getting blackout drunk and doing it all over again the next day, to standing up for a friend when no one else will. Love in the Big City understands that sometimes your soulmate isn't the person you fall in love with. **4/5**

12. *No Other Choice*   
My most anticipated Asian movie of the year slightly underwhelms. One of the weaker works of Park Chan-Wook's career, it's a case of style over substance. Like all of his works, it's sharply shot and edited, teeming with black humor, but it all feels overly programmed, too cynical, and ultimately hollow. Maybe it's the clout of its two megastar leads, or the Western novel it's adapted from, lost in translation. It lacks the emotional pull of *The Handmaiden* and *Decision to Leave*, without the gonzo madness of *Oldboy*. What's left is something in the middle. It reminds me of the first 2/3 of *Lady Vengeance*, but without cathartic ensemble payoff. If I sound harsh, it's because my bar is high. Park is one of my favorite Korean directors (#2 in fact), and he's coming off a hot streak. Overall, it's confident and stylish, but lacking soul. **3.5/5**

13. *Twilight of the Warriors: Walled In*   
*The Yellow Sea* meets *The Raid*. A love letter to Hong Kong action cinema of the 80s and 90s, and a passing of the torch to the next generation of filmmakers. Filled with brutal action, a unique setting, well-developed characters, and a legendary cast. What more could you ask for in a genre film? Be sure to stick around for the end credits, which might be my favorite part of the whole movie. **3.5/5**

14. *A Normal Family*   
A propulsive plot and a strong central moral question, underscored by an undercurrent of impending violence, culminate in a pitch-perfect ending that makes this one of the best recent South Korean thrillers. Some shoddy CGI and a lack of emotional depth keep it from all-time greatness. **3.5/5**

15. *Black Dog*   
Black Dog bears all the hallmarks of a traditional Western: the world-weary drifter with a violent past, his trusty steed reimagined as the Black Dog and motorcycle, the prostitute (here a belly dancer) with a heart of gold, the frontier desert setting, sweeping shots of barren landscape, and the death throes of old traditions giving way to an encroaching civilization. The story follows a taciturn ex-con who returns to his decaying hometown rife with corruption. There, he forms a bond with a wild stray dog, reconciles with his alcoholic father, atones for a past crime, and joins a dog-capturing team led by a local crime boss (played by the great Jia Zhangke). If this seems like a lot, it's because it is. The film juggles too many things at once, brushing up against multiple subplots, but never finding a narrative throughline. Still, you have to admire this Neo-Western's scope. A lesser filmmaker might have leaned harder on genre conventions, but Black Dog retains its humanistic compassion. **3.5/5**

16. *Left-Handed Girl*   
There's much to like here, the standout being the guerrilla-style filmmaking that captures the hustle and bustle of Taipei, transforming the metropolis into a central character. You can feel Baker's influence from the iPhone shot *Tangerine* to the lived-in authenticity of *The Florida Project*. What holds this film back is the contrived melodrama, which undercuts a great performance from newcomer Ma Shih-yuan and the messy subplots that detract from the main narrative. I found myself wishing that the filmmakers had followed the path of *The Florida Project* or *Where Is the Friend's House?*, committing fully to the perspective of the titular character, contrasting a child's innocence with the cruelty of the adult world. **3.5/5**

17. *Santosh*   
What sets this crime procedural apart is its attention to detail, ambitious themes, deliberate pacing, and the perspective of its female titular character. At its best, it plays like *Chinatown* set in rural north India. At its worst, the film casts too wide a net, brushing up against interesting ideas without fully exploring them. At times, it feels more intellectual than emotional, relegating Santosh to a passive observer. Still, its ambition is admirable, and Shahana Goswami delivers an excellent performance. **3.5/5**

18. *Good News*   
A political black comedy and historical satire in the same vein as *The Death of Stalin*. It lacks Iannucci’s relentless bite, but the absurdity lands often enough to justify the ride. Sul Kyung-gu steals the show as the character Nobody. **3/5**

19. *Cloud*   
The first 2/3 of this film is an excellent rendition of a paranoid 70s thriller updated for modern times. The last 30 minutes go off the rails, pivoting into a madcap Western shoot 'em up that undercuts much of what came before. **3/5**

20. *Flat Girls*   
A coming-of-age tale about two childhood friends growing up in a police housing complex as they struggle to transition into adulthood. Tonally, think a lighter Koreeda meets *The Way Way Back*, with the narrative spine of *Soulmate*. While competently made, the film feels derivative, bordering on saccharine. It leans on a soft piano and acoustic score that overstates the emotion. An unnecessary love triangle bloats the runtime well past its welcome, suggesting a lack of confidence in the characters. The good? Bangkok has never looked better on screen, and the assured touch of a debut director hints at real promise. **3/5**

Have you seen any of these? Open to discussion or recs!

## 2. The female patients in Eyes Wide Shut

**Score:** 39
**Body:** So I was rewatching the movie yesterday and when Bill is examining the female patients, I noticed they're all topless. 

However when he is examining two other male patients (one of whom is a kid), they're not topless/expected to be naked. 

Was this supposed to be some not-so-subtle hint that Bill isn't as professional as a doctor as he seems? 

It just seems kinda odd that none of the other scenes with male patients include them being half nude and I couldn't help but wonder if maybe, just maybe, his wife had a point about his female patients being attracted to him and him being aware of that on some level and leaning into on purpose during these examinations

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t think the point is that Bill is unprofessional in a straightforward sense. Kubrick is very precise about perspective. Those examinations aren’t presented as objective medical reality, but as filtered through Bill’s subjectivity. The female patients’ nudity functions less as eroticism and more as a quiet reminder that Bill moves through the world assuming neutrality while constantly being surrounded by desire, vulnerability, and power dynamics he doesn’t fully acknowledge.

The contrast with male patients isn’t about medical protocol, but about what Bill sees versus what he believes himself to be. That gap between self-image and unconscious participation is one of the film’s central tensions, and it directly echoes Alice’s accusation that he underestimates both women’s desire and his own position within it.
  - Just to add - almost all women and girls are red haired  like his wife and daughter (i cant recall the hair of the female patients). 

With his daughter, he doesnt show much involvement, where his wife reads the book, does maths, attends to her. But then the boy in the Dr's office gets his close attention.

So as well as the adult, sexual dynamics, there are also the gendered dynamics that link the red-haired women and girls as sharing common experience, sexualised from youth to maturity, but also objectified, including being seen as lesser or disposable (child being pimped by dad, daughter being neglected, woman who OD'd being killed/raped).

## 3. Does regionalism matter in films?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** As someone from Southern California, it seems like a lot of what Hollywood makes, both film and television, is basically "California" or "Los Angeles" when it comes to regionalism. The major exception is New York City, which definitely has its own unique feel in films and shows. This is not a coincidence because NYC has their own film industry, which means the city can be represented more accurately. 

I understand producers like to "water down/ whitewash" regions to make sure the audience doesn't get alienated, make sure it has a “national feeling”, but in my opinion, this just limits artistic expressions. I actually think it’s worse for TV shows. 

Woody Allen liked to make films about upper class New Yorkers. PTA with Los Angeles. Coen Brothers with various regions of the US. 

I honestly think a film is enhanced when it tries to be accurate to where it takes place, but does this even matter? 

For me it’s the worst when it’s ambiguous “suburban neighborhood or Midwest town” while it’s rare for cities to be portrayed this way. It gets lazy and boring after a while, so why I even started to care. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think the idea of an anonymous suburban neighborhood is exactly that: life is so dull there that they all give you the same feeling. That’s how it works in suburban gothic as a genre, with American Beauty as a representative example, at least. You can definitely instill a sense of place into your movie, though, and tell the story of your own neighborhood, if you want to.
2. My assumption is that it's a matter of budget as often as not, that it costs more to either shoot on location and/or to create regional specificity via production design. I could be wrong.


For me rich details certainly are a plus, though not every film needs them to work. Even though a lot of stuff is shot in CA for example, I don't necessarily interpret the story as Californian of there aren't any clear signifiers. (For that, it helps to not be from the place, I suppose.)
3. *Fargo* is definitely the strongest example I can think of for when a specific region really does enhance the film. I think it would still be as beloved as it is because of the writing and performances but think it would feel just a bit emptier if it was just an unnamed generic American town. 
4. The physical environment has mostly been a semiotic space in American mainstream cinema: the city, the suburb, the woods, the desert, the coast, the road, the jungle. 

I think this is one of its key strengths: the repetition of forms and ideas tell audiences very quickly some baseline assumptions about what they are about to see, especially when mixed with other formal elements that manipulate viewing such as music.

American literary regionalism of the likes of Faulkner, Dreiser, Norris, et. al. had a particular moment that was bound up in the paroxysm of change from the old world to the modern one; one's region accounted for one's fortunes and expectations in life.

Of course there are films that are inextricably bound up with a locale: *Blue Collar* is quite specifically a Detroit film, *Sorry to Bother You* is specially Oakland, whilst nearly all of Sean Baker's latter works have place very much at their heart.

It's no coincidence that these films (and others I am sure you can name) and literary regionalism have some basic leftist principles at their heart, and all to a certain extent meditate on the use of space for particular goals.

European cinema has generally always been much more involved with regionalism as its cinema veers closer to literary notions. It is always relevant that Bruno Dumont's work takes place in the Nord region of France, or Paolo Sorrentino's films reflect Rome rather than Italy, or the Shane Meadows' work takes place in a generalised Midlands.

I quite like that we have cinemas that do both things, and you get magic happening when films like *Bad Day at Black Rock* come along and play with the history of a place (shot near Lone Pine, home of both many historic Western shooting locations and an internment camp for Japanese Americans) in a way that is both specific and phantasmic.
  - > European cinema has generally always been much more involved with regionalism as its cinema veers closer to literary notions.

Thinking about British films of the 2000s that come to mind for me, most of them are closely linked to place. There's The Outrun, mostly set on the remote Orkney Islands (with some great accents that mix Scottish and Scandinavian). The Levelling, set in rural Somerset in the aftermath of the devastating 2014 floods.

Or, to pick a very different example, 24-Hour Party People, about the Manchester music scene.

These films are explicitly about *place* though, so maybe it's all bias in my film preferences.

The one exception that comes to mind is Hoard, which I think could be set in almost any city or town.
5. Why does cinema have to conform to a viewers interpretation of place? Aren’t suburbs ambiguous by nature? They certainly reflect a section of America.

You make an assumption about watering down locations but where is your evidence?

If the issue is stereotypes then that would be a more salient point to address.
  - It's about culture and perspective. A new, stark location also gives a movie a different flavour as opposed to just seeing the same boring locations like los angeles or new york. Theres a bigger world out there.
  - Have you heard of the “Hollywood South”?… Deliverance 😳

TV shows are generally worse, but for films my example is always Casino. 

I understand people on the East Coast probably saw Las Vegas back then as basically “cowboy country” and full of hustlers and cheaters, but the film basically confirms this view by portraying all the natives this way. Also the whole “Vegas was controlled by the mob once” is an oversimplified myth. 

For TV shows, from the Walking Dead to Parks and Recreation to even Breaking Bad. Compare to something like The Wire or Master of None. 

Sometimes it’s not even about predictable stereotypes or accuracy because Do the Right Thing is definitely very interestingly stylized. “Sesame Street of the ghetto” but who cares, the film takes full advantages of its setting to be more unique, rather than the same drugs, crimes, poverty, etc.
6. canadian here. I want to see more canadian movies instead of being struck in the head by americana all the time. I thought Marty Supreme and One Battle After Another were pretty good but not exactly what im in the mood for right now. I absolutely couldn't handle Warfare or Sinners due to their americana. 

It probably doesnt sell as well throughout the rest of the world so we dont get it as much so I get why they dont come out very often. Heated Rivalry was a nice stage for us though. 
  - >canadian here. I want to see more canadian movies instead of being struck in the head by americana all the time.

## 5. Iron Lung (SPOILERS) Review

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Markipiler’s (Mark Fischbach) Iron Lung is a newly released video game adaptation of the same name created by David Szymanski. It is a claustrophobic, Lovecraftian descent into psychological madness. 

Mark Fischbach absolutely steals the show in his role as Simon, a convict sent inside a submarine nicknamed Iron Lung, to explore the unknown depths of a blood ocean located on a mysterious moon, to uncover the secrets it holds in exchange for his freedom. 

(SPOILERS) Simon once lived on the space station known as Eden, he was charged for multiple murders that he did not commit and is unfortunately tasked to go on this mission as a “volunteer” which he did not ask for. Simon endures a lot of physical and emotional strain on himself being caged inside one setting for an infinite amount of hours, maybe even days. As time passes he loses his grip on reality and hallucinates and can’t tell the difference between reality or deception.. which leads him to go full on ape shit and understandably so I mean who wouldn’t lose their fucking mind being stuck in a submarine in space? Especially if something is fucking watching you and you have no clue what the fuck it is or where your submarine is headed towards. 

(SPOILERS) The film kicks off with an impressively ominous monologue regarding a universal apocalyptic event known as “The Quiet Rapture,” a  mysterious catastrophe where stars and inhabitable planets vanished without any warning or trace, plunging the universe into darkness. Basically leaving humanity stranded in space among barren moons, prompting desperate exploration into oceans of blood found on some of these moons. 

The use of special effects both being practical and CGI are awesome and very well done. Myself and my buddy Dylan were blown away by the makeup effects and how far Markipiler went in terms of capturing a gory unpleasant body horror experience trust us it pays off you just need a little more patience. The blood itself is not in the background but in the foreground as a character in its own right which definitely amplifies the claustrophobic, vibe and unpleasantness of being stuck in one setting. There’s other cool gory things in store but if you still can go out and support this indie horror film on the big screen. 

The pacing may turn off some people or make them walk away but for myself and Dylan while we did notice some issues with the pacing in the end, the payoff was 100% worth our time and money seeing Iron Lung. 

Another issue that we think people will have is the audio from a technical standpoint however, from a narrative standpoint you can argue that it is meant to be distorted given what is happening around Simon on screen. 

Myself and Dylan can’t express how impressed we are for such a huge YouTuber / Let’s Player like Markiplier to have the balls to go out there and start funding, writing, directing and editing his own movie. How many people nowadays have the courage to make their own movies? Not very fucking many. 

It’s truly a remarkable achievement to get your movie in theatres that’s made by you. We enjoyed it and that is all that matters. We give Iron Lung a B for Bloody Good. We wouldn’t mind owning this in our Blu-Ray / 4K Collection and wouldn’t mind seeing another film from Markipiler.
